explanation.title: How print() Works and Why Comments Matter
explanation.content:
# The print() Function

Think of `print()` as Python's loudspeaker. Anything you hand it, it broadcasts to the screen. You have already used it once, but it is capable of much more than displaying a single line of text.

## Printing Different Kinds of Values

Python can print several kinds of data:

```python
print("Hello")       # a string — text in quotes
print(42)            # an integer — a whole number
print(3.14)          # a float — a decimal number
print(True)          # a boolean — True or False
```

Each call to `print()` adds a **newline** at the end, so the next output starts on a fresh line.

## Controlling the Separator and End Character

By default, `print()` puts a space between multiple items and a newline at the end. You can change both:

```python
print("Name", "Age", sep=" | ")   # Name | Age
print("Loading", end="...")        # Loading... (no newline)
print(" done")                     # Loading... done
```

- `sep` sets the string placed **between** items (default is one space)
- `end` sets the string placed **after** the last item (default is `"\n"`, a newline)

## Printing with f-strings

An **f-string** lets you embed variable values directly inside a string. Place `f` immediately before the opening quote, then put variable names inside `{}` braces:

```python
name  = "Rahul"
marks = 88
print(f"{name} scored {marks} out of 100.")
```

Output: `Rahul scored 88 out of 100.`

## Comments — Notes in Your Code

A **comment** starts with `#` and runs to the end of the line. Python ignores comments entirely. You should use them to:

- Explain *why* you wrote a piece of code
- Leave a note for your future self
- Temporarily disable a line while testing

```python
# This calculates the discounted price
price    = 500
discount = 0.10        # 10% off
final    = price - (price * discount)
print(final)           # expected: 450.0
```

Well-commented code is a mark of a professional programmer. In the code example below, you will see `print()` used with `sep`, `end`, and an f-string together.

codeExample.title: print() Options Showcase
codeExample.code:
# Demonstrate the many ways print() can display output

# 1. Basic print — adds newline at end automatically
print("Line one")
print("Line two")

# 2. Print multiple items — default separator is one space
print("Python", "3", "is", "great")

# 3. Custom separator between items
print("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", sep="-")

# 4. Custom end character — stays on same line
print("Connecting", end="")
print("...")
print("Connected!")

# 5. f-string — embed variable values inside text
course = "Python"
level  = "Beginner"
print(f"Welcome to {course} {level}!")

# 6. Arithmetic result inside f-string
year_started = 1991
age_of_python = 2025 - year_started
print(f"Python has been around for {age_of_python} years.")
codeExample.explanation:
- `print("Line one")` — the simplest form; displays text and moves to the next line automatically
- `sep="-"` — replaces the default space separator; the five day names appear as `Mon-Tue-Wed-Thu-Fri`
- `end=""` — replaces the default newline with an empty string, so the next `print()` continues on the same line
- `f"Welcome to {course} {level}!"` — an **f-string**; variables inside `{}` are substituted with their values before the string is displayed
- `2025 - year_started` inside an f-string — Python evaluates the expression first and inserts the result

exercise.title: Build a Formatted Name Badge
exercise.instructions:
Create a program that prints a name badge for a student. Define two variables: name (a string) and batch (a string such as '2024'). Then print three lines: a separator line of 30 dashes, the badge content using an f-string that says 'Name: [name] | Batch: [batch]', and another separator line of 30 dashes. The dashes must be printed using the * operator: print('-' * 30).

Expected output:
------------------------------
Name: Rahul | Batch: 2024
------------------------------
exercise.starterCode:
# Name Badge Generator

# Step 1 — define the variables
name  = "Rahul"
batch = "2024"

# Step 2 — print the top border (30 dashes)
print("-" * 30)

# Step 3 — print the badge content using an f-string
# Your code here

# Step 4 — print the bottom border (30 dashes)
# Your code here
exercise.solutionCode:
# Name Badge Generator

name  = "Rahul"
batch = "2024"

print("-" * 30)
print(f"Name: {name} | Batch: {batch}")
print("-" * 30)
hints: 💡 Use an f-string: start the string with f before the quote, then put variable names inside curly braces like {name}.
💡 The separator line is already shown as print("-" * 30) — the * operator repeats the dash character 30 times.
💡 The badge line should look like f"Name: {name} | Batch: {batch}" — both variables go inside their own pair of curly braces.

questions.question: What does print("A", "B", "C", sep="-") display?
questions.explanation: The sep keyword argument sets the string placed between each item in a print() call. With sep="-", Python places a dash between each adjacent item, producing A-B-C. The default separator is a single space, which is why print("A", "B") normally shows A B.

questions.question: Which line correctly uses an f-string to display 'Score: 95'?
questions.explanation: An f-string requires the letter f immediately before the opening quote: f"...". Variables are placed inside curly braces {}. Without the f prefix, Python treats the curly braces as literal characters and prints Score: {score} rather than the variable's value. Concatenation with + also works but f-strings are the modern, preferred approach.

questions.question: What does the end parameter in print() control?
options: The separator placed between multiple items
The number of items you can print
The character or string placed after the last item instead of a newline
The colour of the printed text
questions.explanation: The end parameter replaces the default newline character that print() adds after the last item. Setting end="" prevents moving to a new line, so the next print() continues on the same line. Setting end="\n\n" would add an extra blank line after each call. The sep parameter (not end) controls what goes between items.
